request.question: Which president was present for the Mexican Civil War who originated from Oaxaca?
request.answer: liberal politician of Zapotec origin from Oaxaca.

title: 128th Infantry Regiment (United States)
José María Lafragua
The Epic of American Civilization
History of Oaxaca
The Old Gringo
Aureliano Blanquet
Mexican Youth Athenaeum
Benito Juárez
Agnes Salm-Salm
George Puflea
sentences: The 128th Infantry Regiment ("Les Terribles") is a United States military unit of the Wisconsin National Guard.
The 128th has served as part of the American Civil War, Spanish–American War, Mexican Civil War, World War I, World War II and the Iraq War.
José María Ibarra Lafragua was a Mexican liberal lawyer, politician, diplomat, and writer.
He was born in Puebla, 2 April 1813, the son of Lt. Col. José María Lafragua and Mariana de Ibarra and Veytia.
He served in as Minister of Interior in the government of liberal President Ignacio Comonfort.
He was the first director of the National Library of Mexico and key author of the Mexican Civil and Penal Codes.
The Epic of American Civilization is a mural by the social realist painter José Clemente Orozco.
It is located in the basement reading room of the Baker Memorial Library on the campus of Dartmouth College in Hanover, New Hampshire.

The mural, painted between 1932 and 1934, consists of a series of 24 fresco panels, whose principal themes are the impact of both indigenous Native Americans and European colonists on North America, and the impact of war (particularly the Mexican Civil War and the First World War) and rapid industrialization on the human spirit.
The mural was designated a National Historic Landmark in 2013.
In the Central Valley region of the Southern Mexican state of Oaxaca archeologists discovered evidence of historic settlements.

Aztecs from Tenochtitlan on the volcanic plateau to the North around what today is Mexico City first arrived in this region around 1250 AD establishing military rule in the 15th century until the arrival of the Spanish.
After the fall of Tenochtitlan, the Spanish took over Oaxaca which led to the eventual decrease of the Native population and the increase in African slaves.
The region was then settled by mostly Spanish immigrants from Europe and the African slaves they brought with them.
Oaxaca was considered a department after the Mexican War of Independence, but after the fall of emperor Agustín de Iturbide, it became a state in 1824 with José Murguia as its first governor.
During the 19th century, Oaxaca was split between liberal and conservative factions.
The political and military struggles between the factions resulted in wars and intrigues.
A series of major disasters occurred in the state from the 1920s to the 1940s.
In the 1940s and 1950s, new infrastructure projects were begun.
From the 1980s to the present, there has been much development of the tourism industry in the state.
The Old Gringo (Spanish: "Gringo Viejo" ) is a novel by Carlos Fuentes, written from 1964 to 1984 and first published in 1985.
Fuentes stated: "What started this novel was my admiration for Ambrose Bierce and for his "Tales of Soldiers and Civilians".
I was fascinated with the idea of a man who fought in the United States Civil War and dies in a Mexican civil war."
The novel addresses themes of death, cultural exchange, and Mexican identity, among others.
Its English-language translation became the first novel by a Mexican author to become a U.S. bestseller.
The book was one of three nominees for the Ritz Paris Hemingway Award as best novel of 1985.
Aureliano Blanquet (Morelia, Michoacán, 31 December 1849 – 15 April 1919 Barranca de Chavaxtla, Huatusco, Veracruz) was a general of the Federal Army during the Mexican Civil War.
He was a key participant in the coup d'état during the Decena trágica.
Blanquet has been identified "as one of the major villains of the Mexican Revolution".

The Mexican Youth Athenaeum, later known as the "Athenaeum of Mexico", was a Mexican civil association founded on October 28, 1909 with the purpose of working in favor of culture and art, by means of organization public meetings and debates.

Born as a response of a generation of young intellectuals who in the decline of the rule of President Porfirio Díaz set a series of criticisms to determinism and mechanism set by the Comtian and Spencerian positivism as the development model of Porfirio Díaz's administration and the group of the "científicos. Through a series of conferences and different cultural efforts they activated a reflexive awareness on education.
Benito Pablo Juárez García (] ; 21 March 1806 – 18 July 1872) was a Mexican lawyer and liberal politician of Zapotec origin from Oaxaca.

Agnes Salm-Salm (December 25, 1844 – December 21, 1912) was the American wife of Prince Felix zu Salm-Salm, a Prussian mercenary beside whom she played a role in the American Civil War, the Mexican Civil War between President Benito Juárez and the Austrian archduke Maximilian I of Mexico, and the Franco-Prussian War.
George Puflea Craciun was an early aviator.
Born in modern-day Romania, he flew in the First Balkan War, in the Mexican Revolution, in the First World War and in early American and Mexican civil aviation.
He was also known under his name in Spanish, .

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.question: Which president was present for the Mexican Civil War who originated from Oaxaca?
response.gold_answer: liberal politician of Zapotec origin from Oaxaca.

response.hop1_query: Which president was present for the Mexican Civil War who originated from Oaxaca?
hop1_docs: History of Oaxaca | In the Central Valley region of the Southern Mexican state of Oaxaca archeologists discovered evidence of historic settlements.  Aztecs from Tenochtitlan on the volcanic plateau to the North around what today is Mexico City first arrived in this region around 1250 AD establishing military rule in the 15th century until the arrival of the Spanish.  After the fall of Tenochtitlan, the Spanish took over Oaxaca which led to the eventual decrease of the Native population and the increase in African slaves.  The region was then settled by mostly Spanish immigrants from Europe and the African slaves they brought with them.  Oaxaca was considered a department after the Mexican War of Independence, but after the fall of emperor Agustín de Iturbide, it became a state in 1824 with José Murguia as its first governor.  During the 19th century, Oaxaca was split between liberal and conservative factions.  The political and military struggles between the factions resulted in wars and intrigues.  A series of major disasters occurred in the state from the 1920s to the 1940s.  In the 1940s and 1950s, new infrastructure projects were begun.  From the 1980s to the present, there has been much development of the tourism industry in the state.

Capture of Oaxaca (1812) | The Capture of Oaxaca was a battle during the War of Mexican Independence that occurred on 25 November 1812 at Oaxaca, Oaxaca.  It was fought between the royalist forces loyal to the Spanish crown, commanded by General Antonio Pío González-Saravia Mollinedo, and the Mexican rebels fighting for independence from the Spanish Empire, commanded by José María Morelos y Pavón.  The battle resulted in a victory for the Mexican rebels.

Félix Díaz (politician) | Félix Díaz Velasco (17 February 18689 July 1945) was a Mexican politician and general born in Oaxaca, Oaxaca.  He graduated as an engineer from the Colegio Militar in 1888.  He was a leading figure in the rebellion against President Francisco I. Madero during the Mexican Revolution.

Marquisate of the Valley of Oaxaca | The Marquisate of the Valley of Oaxaca (Spanish: "Marquesado del Valle de Oaxaca" ) is a hereditary marquisal title in the Spanish nobility and a former seignorial estate in New Spain.  It was granted to Don Hernán Cortés, "conquistador" who led the conquest of the Aztec Empire, by Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor in 1529.  Despite its name, the marquisate covered a much larger area than the Valley of Oaxaca, comprising a vast stretch of land in the present-day Mexican states of Oaxaca, Morelos, Veracruz, Michoacán and Mexico.

Copa de Zaachila | The Copa de Zaachila is presented each year by the President of the Mexican Baseball League to the team that wins the Serie del Rey.  Recent trophy designs contain the design of a china cup found in the archeological zone of Zaachila, Oaxaca.

Libyan Civil War (2011–present) | The Libyan Civil War refers to the ongoing conflicts in Libya, beginning with the Arab Spring protests of 2011, which led to the First Libyan Civil War, foreign military intervention, and the ousting and death of Muammar Gaddafi.  The civil war's aftermath and proliferation of armed groups led to violence and instability across the country, which erupted into renewed civil war in 2014.  The ongoing crisis in Libya has so far resulted in tens of thousands of casualties since the onset of violence in early 2011.  During both civil wars, the output of Libya's economically crucial oil industry collapsed to a small fraction of its usual level, with most facilities blockaded or damaged by rival groups, despite having the largest oil reserves of any African country.  U.S. President Barack Obama stated on 11 April 2016 that not preparing for a post-Gaddafi Libya was probably the "worst mistake" of his presidency.

Monte Albán | Monte Albán, likely originally called Danipaguache (Zapotec for sacred mountain of life), is a large pre-Columbian archaeological site in the Santa Cruz Xoxocotlán Municipality in the southern Mexican state of Oaxaca (17.043° N, 96.767°W).  The site is located on a low mountainous range rising above the plain in the central section of the Valley of Oaxaca where the latter's northern Etla, eastern Tlacolula, and southern Zimatlán & Ocotlán (or Valle Grande) branches meet.  The present-day state capital Oaxaca City is located approximately 9 km east of Monte Albán.
hop1_titles: Marquisate of the Valley of Oaxaca
response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - Félix Díaz — Passage [3] "Félix Díaz (politician)": "born in Oaxaca, Oaxaca" and "leading figure in the rebellion against President Francisco I. Madero" (Mexican Revolution).
  - Capture of Oaxaca — Passage [2]: mentions José María Morelos y Pavón (commander, 1812) but does not identify a president from Oaxaca.
  - History of Oaxaca — Passage [1]: mentions José Murguia as first governor (no president cited).
  - No passage mentions Benito Juárez or Porfirio Díaz (both known historically as presidents from Oaxaca).

- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - Unclear which "Mexican Civil War" the question means (Reform War, Mexican Revolution, War of Independence-era conflicts, etc.).
  - No passage provides a named Mexican president from Oaxaca who is explicitly stated to have been "present" in any civil war.

- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - Identify which civil war is intended (name and date range).
  - For that war, confirm any Mexican president who (a) was born in Oaxaca and (b) was present/involved in that conflict (name, birth origin, and role/participation).

- retrieval_direction (priority order):
  1. Page: "Benito Juárez" — check birthplace (Oaxaca) and involvement in the Reform War / French Intervention.
  2. Page: "Porfirio Díaz" — check birthplace (Oaxaca) and role relative to the Mexican Revolution.
  3. Query: "List of presidents of Mexico from Oaxaca" or "Mexican presidents born in Oaxaca".
  4. Query: "Mexican Civil War" + specify candidate war names (e.g., "Reform War 1857–1861", "Mexican Revolution 1910–1920") to map presidents' participation.
response.hop2_query: Benito Juárez Porfirio Díaz born in Oaxaca involvement Reform War French Intervention Mexican Revolution
hop2_docs: Felipe Berriozábal | Felipe Berriozábal (born August 23, 1829 in Zacatecas, Zacatecas – died January 9, 1900 in Mexico City) was a Mexican politician, engineer and military leader.  He participated in the Reform War (Spanish: "Guerra de Reforma" ) and in the fight against French Intervention in Mexico.  He was a member of president Benito Juárez's cabinet, serving as Secretary of War and Secretary of Marine, Berriozábal also served during Porfirio Díaz's government.  He was a commander of the Mexican Army and member of the Chamber of Deputies.  His remains were buried at the Panteón de Dolores in Mexico City, in the Rotunda of Illustrious Persons on January 12, 1900; shortly after his death.

Battle of Miahuatlán | The Battle of Miahuatlán took place on 3 October 1866 in the vicinity of the current municipality of Miahuatlán de Porfirio Díaz in the state of Oaxaca, Mexico.  It was fought between elements of the Mexican republican army under General Porfirio Díaz and troops of the Second Mexican Empire during the French intervention in Mexico.

Plan de la Noria | The Plan de la Noria was a revolutionary call to arms with the intent of ousting Mexican President Benito Juárez.  The plan was drafted by Porfirio Díaz immediately following his defeat by Juárez in the presidential election of 1871.  Neither Juárez, Díaz, nor the third candidate Sebastián Lerdo de Tejada won the majority of votes.  The vote went to the Mexican Congress which, being full of Juáristas, elected Juárez to his fourth term.  Díaz proclaimed himself in revolt and drafted the Plan de la Noria in which he demanded electoral freedom and no re-election.  He gained few supporters and was temporarily defeated in Oaxaca, where his brother Felix was killed Benito Juárez died of a heart attack in July 1872 and Chief Justice Sebastián Lerdo succeeded the presidency, as per the stipulation laid forth in the constitution of 1857.  Lerdo ran for re-election in 1876 which gave Díaz another chance to revolt, and this time successfully under the Plan de Tuxtepec.

Diódoro Corella | Diódoro Corella (born 1838 in Arizpe, Sonora - died June 15, 1876 in Mexico City) was a Mexican military leader.  Corella was a republican and strongly opposed French Intervention in Mexico, reason why he was exiled by the conservative government.  He returned to Mexico upon the victory of Benito Juárez and fought against Porfirio Díaz when he tried to rise to power.  His remains were interred at the Panteón de Dolores in Mexico City, in the Rotunda of Illustrious Persons on June 17, 1876.

Protasio Tagle | Protasio Tagle was a Mexican soldier and politician who lived in the 19th century.  He participated in the Reform War as a member of the liberal sector, and later led a republican division during the second French intervention in Mexico.  He joined general Porfirio Díaz during the Revolution of Tuxtepec (see Plan de Tuxtepec) and, after Díaz won and became the president of Mexico, Tagle was appointed as the Secretary of the Interior.  In 1879 there were possibilities for him to be the presidential candidate, but he was defeated by Manuel González and finally retired from political activities.

Sebastián Lerdo de Tejada | Sebastián Lerdo de Tejada y Corral (] ; 24 April 1823 – 21 April 1889) was a jurist and Liberal president of Mexico, succeeding Benito Juárez who died of a heart attack in July 1872.  Lerdo was elected to his own presidential term later in 1872 rather than remaining successor due to his previous office of Chief Justice of the Supreme Court.  Juárez's political rival liberal General Porfirio Díaz had attempted a coup against Juárez, but his Plan de la Noria failed and Díaz eliminated as a political foe during Lerdo's 1872-76 term, giving Lerdo considerable leeway to pursue his program without political interference.  Lerdo was more successful than Juárez in his final years as president in pacifying the country and strengthening the Mexican state.  He ran for another term in 1876 and was elected, but was overthrown by Porfirio Díaz and his supporters under the Plan of Tuxtepec, which asserted the principle of no-reelection to the presidency.  Lerdo died in exile in New York in 1889, but Díaz invited the return of his body to Mexico for burial with full honors.  Not counting Miguel Miramón, an unrecognized president during the Reform War, he is the first president of the recognized presidents that was not born during Spanish colonial rule.

Porfirio Díaz | José de la Cruz Porfirio Díaz Mori (] ; 15 September 1830 – 2 July 1915) was a Mexican general and politician who served seven terms as President of Mexico, a total of three and a half decades from 1876 to 1880 and from 1884 to 1911.  A veteran of the War of the Reform (1858–60) and the French intervention in Mexico (1862–67), Díaz rose to the rank of General, leading republican troops against the French-imposed rule of Emperor Maximilian.  Seizing power in a coup in 1876, Díaz and his allies, a group of technocrats known as "Científicos", ruled Mexico for the next thirty-five years, a period known as the "Porfiriato".
response.summary_2: Benito Juárez and Porfirio Díaz. Benito Juárez was the president during the Reform War (the mid‑19th‑century Mexican civil war). Porfirio Díaz was a veteran of the War of the Reform and later served multiple terms as President of Mexico.
